meta.name: IRI Empathy Scale
meta.description: The IRI is a 28-item self-report measure of empathy assessing perspective taking, fantasy, empathic concern, and personal distress.
meta.author: Davis MH (1983)
meta.reference: Davis MH. Measuring individual differences in empathy. J Pers Soc Psychol. 1983;44(1):113-126.
meta.instruction: Indicate how well each statement describes you.

questions.text: I daydream and fantasize, with some regularity, about things that might happen to me.
I often have tender, concerned feelings for people less fortunate than me.
I sometimes find it difficult to see things from the other guy’s point of view.
Sometimes I don’t feel very sorry for other people when they are having problems.
I really get involved with the feelings of the characters in a novel.
In emergency situations, I feel apprehensive and ill-at-ease.
I am usually objective when I watch a movie or play, and I don’t often get completely caught up in it.
I try to look at everybody’s side of a disagreement before I make a decision.
When I see someone being taken advantage of, I feel kind of protective towards them.
I sometimes feel helpless when I am in the middle of a very emotional situation.
I sometimes try to understand my friends better by imagining how things look from their perspective.
Becoming extremely involved in a good book or movie is somewhat rare for me.
When I see someone get hurt, I tend to remain calm.
Other people’s misfortunes do not usually disturb me a great deal.
If I’m sure I’m right about something, I don’t waste much time listening to other people’s arguments.
After seeing a play or movie, I have felt as though I were one of the characters.
Being in a tense emotional situation scares me.
When I see someone being treated unfairly, I sometimes don’t feel very much pity for them.
I am usually pretty effective in dealing with emergencies.
I am often quite touched by things that I see happen.
I believe that there are two sides to every question and try to look at them both.
I would describe myself as a pretty soft-hearted person.
When I watch a good movie, I can very easily put myself in the place of a leading character.
I tend to lose control during emergencies.
When I’m upset at someone, I usually try to put myself in his shoes for a while.
When I am reading an interesting story or novel, I imagine how I would feel if the events were happening to me.
When I see someone who badly needs help in an emergency, I go to pieces.
Before criticizing somebody, I try to imagine how I would feel if I were in their place.

ranges.label: Low Empathy
ranges.description: Your overall empathy score is low, suggesting limited tendency to take others’ perspectives, feel concern for others, or share in others’ emotional experiences. While empathy exists on a spectrum, developing empathic skills can improve relationships and social functioning.
suggestions: Practice active listening — focus fully on understanding others’ perspectives before responding
Read fiction or watch films and try to deeply consider characters’ inner experiences
Engage in volunteer work or community service to broaden exposure to diverse life experiences
Consider whether low empathy is impacting your relationships and seek professional guidance if needed

ranges.label: Moderate Empathy
ranges.description: Your empathy is in the moderate range. You show some capacity for understanding and sharing others’ emotional experiences. Review your subscale scores to see which dimensions of empathy are stronger or weaker for you.
suggestions: Review subscale scores to identify empathy dimensions you can strengthen
Practice perspective-taking by actively imagining how others experience situations
Develop emotional vocabulary to better recognize and understand others’ feelings
Engage in conversations where you prioritize understanding over responding

ranges.label: High Empathy
ranges.description: Your empathy is well-developed across multiple dimensions. You readily understand and share others’ emotional experiences. While this is generally beneficial for relationships, ensure you maintain healthy emotional boundaries to prevent empathic burnout.
suggestions: Practice healthy emotional boundaries to prevent compassion fatigue
Use your strong empathy skills to support others while protecting your own well-being
If personal distress subscale is high, learn grounding techniques to manage emotional contagion

perspective_taking.label: Low
perspective_taking.description: Your tendency to spontaneously adopt others’ viewpoints is low. Perspective-taking is a cognitive empathy skill that can be developed through deliberate practice, improving communication and conflict resolution.

perspective_taking.label: Moderate
perspective_taking.description: You show moderate ability to consider others’ points of view. Continuing to practice seeing situations from multiple perspectives can enhance your interpersonal effectiveness and social understanding.

perspective_taking.label: High
perspective_taking.description: You readily adopt others’ psychological perspectives, which supports effective communication, conflict resolution, and relationship quality. This cognitive empathy skill is valuable in both personal and professional contexts.

fantasy.label: Low
fantasy.description: You have a limited tendency to imagine yourself in fictional characters’ situations. While not problematic, engaging more with narratives can stimulate imagination and indirectly enhance empathic understanding.

fantasy.label: Moderate
fantasy.description: You moderately engage with fictional characters’ experiences. This imaginative capacity can serve as a safe practice ground for understanding diverse emotional perspectives and life situations.

fantasy.label: High
fantasy.description: You strongly identify with fictional characters, readily imagining their thoughts and feelings. This rich imaginative capacity can enhance emotional understanding, though very high levels may sometimes blur fiction and reality.

empathic_concern.label: Low
empathic_concern.description: Your tendency to feel warmth, concern, and compassion for others in distress is low. Low empathic concern may affect relationship quality. Volunteering and exposure to others’ experiences can help develop compassionate responding.

empathic_concern.label: Moderate
empathic_concern.description: You show moderate feelings of concern and sympathy for others experiencing difficulties. This other-oriented emotional response is central to prosocial behavior and healthy relationships.

empathic_concern.label: High
empathic_concern.description: You demonstrate strong feelings of warmth and concern for others in distress. High empathic concern motivates helping behavior and strengthens social bonds, and is a key component of compassionate care.

personal_distress.label: Low
personal_distress.description: You experience minimal personal distress when witnessing others’ negative experiences. Low personal distress indicates good emotional regulation and the ability to remain composed while still being empathic.

personal_distress.label: Moderate
personal_distress.description: You experience moderate personal distress in response to others’ difficult situations. Some self-oriented anxiety in empathic situations is normal, but if it interferes with helping behavior, grounding techniques may help.

personal_distress.label: High
personal_distress.description: You experience significant personal distress when witnessing others’ negative experiences. High personal distress can be overwhelming and may actually impair your ability to help others. Developing emotional regulation and grounding skills is recommended.
